name: Hello Robot Stretch
short_description: A compact, lightweight, low-cost mobile manipulator with a telescoping arm, designed for homes, research, and assistive use.
long_description: Stretch is a wheeled mobile manipulator built around a slim telescoping arm on a lifting mast and a two-wheel differential base, weighing roughly 25 kg so a single person can carry it. Hello Robot, founded by Aaron Edsinger and Charlie Kemp, positioned it as an affordable, open-source platform for mobile manipulation research and for giving people with severe mobility impairments greater independence. It matters for everyday physical AI because it brings manipulation out of the factory and into human living spaces at a price and footprint that ordinary labs and homes can actually accommodate, and it serves as a common embodiment for testing learned manipulation and language-driven control.

images.caption: Hello Robot's Stretch mobile manipulator, a wheeled base with a lifting mast and a telescoping arm.
images.alt_text: A tall, slim white-and-orange mobile robot on a two-wheeled base, with a vertical mast and a thin telescoping arm ending in a gripper.

sources.title: Hello Robot

interactions.title: Scratch an itch you can't reach
interactions.description: Henry Evans, who has quadriplegia, fits a modified hairbrush into Stretch's gripper and drives it to his head to scratch an itch himself, instead of waiting on a caregiver.

interactions.title: Feed yourself a forkful of eggs
interactions.description: Using on-screen controls, a person with limited arm movement positions Stretch's gripper to bring scrambled eggs up to their mouth, taking a bite on their own schedule.

interactions.title: Drive the robot with your head from across the room
interactions.description: Wearing the Head-Worn Assistive Teleoperation rig, a user maps head tilts and speech to the arm and base — no fine hand motion needed — to fetch a drink or open the blinds.

interactions.title: Teach it a chore in five minutes
interactions.description: With NYU's Dobb-E, you record about five minutes of demonstrations using a phone-on-a-stick — opening blinds, pouring marshmallows, closing a drawer — and Stretch learns to repeat the task at home.

interactions.title: Deal a hand of poker, present a rose
interactions.description: Beyond chores, Henry Evans has used Stretch to manipulate playing cards in a poker game with friends and to hand his wife Jane a rose — social gestures, not just utility.

interactions.title: Pilot it from another city
interactions.description: Because the web teleop interface runs over the internet, a remote helper or therapist can log in from anywhere, see the head and gripper camera feeds, and drive the arm to retrieve an object in someone's home.

interactions.title: Ask it in plain language to bring you something
interactions.description: Stretch AI routes a spoken request through an LLM that emits robot API calls, so 'find my mug and bring it here' becomes a navigate-find-grasp-deliver sequence the robot runs on its own.

interactions.title: A standing reminder that gently nudges your hand
interactions.description: Given its force-sensing gripper and a scheduler, Stretch could roll up at a set time, hold out a pill cup, and wait at low force until you take it — extending its proven fetch-and-hold skills into a medication-prompt ritual.
